persona.name: Maya Patel

occupation.title: Software Engineer
occupation.description: Mid-level backend software engineer at Sprinklr's Chicago office, two years into her career. Works primarily in Python and Go on infrastructure tooling. Comfortable in her role and now spending energy on deepening specialization rather than just keeping up. Remote-works two days per week. Attends two standups daily and does thorough code review.

persona.education: B.S. in Computer Science from University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign (2024), GPA 3.7. No formal biology or chemistry background, but applies her engineering mindset — evidence, systems thinking, skepticism of black-box claims — to any domain including skincare.
long_term_goals: Reach senior engineer level in 2 years, then consider a tech lead track
Pay off student loans within 3 years (currently on track)
Improve her skin in a sustainable, evidence-based way — not a product addiction
Learn classical Bharatanatyam dance more seriously
Visit extended family in Ahmedabad and see her parents' hometown
persona.style: Maya is measured, precise, and a little dry. She asks clarifying questions before giving an opinion and uses full sentences without filler words. When she recommends something it's because she's done the research; when she's skeptical she says so plainly: 'I don't see evidence for that claim.' She doesn't gush. Her look is clean, low-maintenance, and professional — neutral colors, minimal makeup, a consistent routine she doesn't have to think about. Her coworkers describe her as sharp and reliable.

traits: Evidence-first thinker — won't adopt a product without understanding its mechanism
Skeptical of marketing claims, especially 'clean beauty' without clinical data
Low-maintenance by design: a 4-product routine she can finish in 3 minutes
Socially reserved but deeply loyal to a small close circle
Prefers primary sources (dermatologist-authored content, PubMed) to influencer recommendations
Can become genuinely engaged about any topic with rigorous science behind it

interests: Dermatologically backed skincare research (reads abstracts, not just headlines)
Personal finance, FIRE math, and early retirement optimization (r/personalfinance)
Classical music and Bharatanatyam dance practice
Backend engineering and distributed systems (conference talks, technical blogs)
Cooking South Indian food from her mom's recipes
Hiking on trips to Colorado to visit relatives
likes: A cleanser and moisturizer that do their job without drama
Products with peer-reviewed citations somewhere on the brand's site
La Roche-Posay and CeraVe — evidence-supported and accessible
Discovering that a cheaper product works equally well
Unsubscribing from every beauty email list she's accidentally joined
Her North Side apartment with a partial lake view
Making dal and rice on Sundays
dislikes: Skincare TikTok — feels like a content churn machine that creates more problems than it solves
Ingredient lists that bury actives too deep to know the concentration
Brands that rely on aspirational marketing instead of clinical claims
Multi-step routines that take more than 5 minutes
Buying something new when what she has still works
'Clean beauty' marketing without defined standards or safety data

beliefs: Most skincare problems are solved by cleansing, moisturizing, and SPF — everything else is marginal
The beauty industry manufactures insecurities to sell solutions
Consistency beats complexity in any system, including skincare
Scientific literacy is a life skill everyone should invest in
Spending $200 on skincare when $25 equivalents exist is paying a marketing tax
skills: Evaluating scientific claims and reading peer-reviewed study abstracts
Identifying data manipulation in marketing materials
Reading INCI lists and understanding active concentration thresholds

general: Uses the same 4 products for months without changing anything unless something stops working
Googles '[product name] clinical study site:pubmed.gov' before trying anything new
Ignores influencer recommendations unless confirmed by a dermatologist
Buys skincare on Amazon to avoid in-store upsell pressure
Has never bought a product because it was trending — only because she evaluated it

morning: 6:15am alarm; phone stays off until after coffee
Quick shower — CeraVe Hydrating Cleanser, Aveeno Daily Moisturizer, La Roche-Posay SPF 50
Dressed and at laptop by 7:45am (remote day) or leaves by 8am (office day)
No makeup except concealer if needed
workday: Lunch at home or reading — no social media scrolling
Afternoon: code review, meetings, technical documentation
evening: CeraVe cleanser + thin moisturizer — done in 90 seconds
Reads engineering blog posts or a book
weekend: Saturday Pilates class or long walk along the lakefront
Grocery run + Sunday meal prep (dal, rice, vegetables)
Video call with parents in Edison, NJ
One social event with a small group of close friends

health.physical: Generally healthy with chronically dry skin that worsens in Chicago winters. Uses a bedroom humidifier and manages dryness with consistent moisturizer use rather than a serum stack. No acne. Gets adequate sleep and eats well.
health.mental: Calm and emotionally steady. Low neuroticism. Occasional low-grade loneliness from being introverted in a city she moved to for work, but she's built a small social network she values. Skincare is maintenance, not an emotional outlet.

relationships.name: Priya Patel
relationships.description: Retired teacher in Edison, NJ. Sends Maya care packages with home-cooked food and Himalaya herbal creams. Maya uses the face cream if it doesn't break her out. They call every Sunday.

relationships.name: Kira
relationships.description: Also a software engineer at Sprinklr, one year senior to Maya. They have lunch together twice a week and have a running bet on who can save more money per quarter.

relationships.name: Rohan Patel
relationships.description: Dermatology resident at Northwestern. The reason Maya knows which evidence actually matters. She texts him product questions instead of Googling. He's told her twice that retinoids and SPF are the only things with clear evidence for aging prevention.
